## [00:00 - 03:03]

All right, let's talk about some of the nuts and bolts of homework for life, the things that are going to get you to be as successful as possible. One of the first and most important things I want you to keep in mind is we don't judge what we write down. We don't deem it as boring or unstoryworthy in any way. We write it down and we move on. There's a perfect example of what I'm talking about. Last May, I had a day where really nothing happened. Six people, three couples, eating hamburgers and hot dogs. That's all I had. It felt so tragically suburban and ordinary. I remember sitting there and thinking, was there really nothing else? There wasn't. One item for the day, I put it down and I moved on, no judgment. Two months later, the neighbors to our left announced they were getting divorced. We never saw it coming. There are friends. We know them really well. They have children and the three backyards are all one backyard. Our kids play in them. They're all friends. Then suddenly the neighbors to the left are getting a divorce. Two days later, the neighbors to our right announced that they too were getting a divorce. In the span of 72 hours, we discovered that the neighbors to the left who were our friends and the neighbors to the right who were our friends were no longer going to be together. That our utopian backyard neighborhood childlike wonderland was now in jeopardy. That eventually became a story that I tell on stages. That story begins on an evening in May when three couples were sitting on a deck eating hamburgers and hot dogs. I thought those couples were all happy couples.
